summary: I found three governance-bypass risks: partial schema renames pass, compile-fix comments can smuggle build directives, and audit grant-count checking does not bind grants to approved IDs.

findings.title: schema_predicate_correction allows partial renames within a predicate
findings.evidence: runSchemaPredicateCheck treats unchanged old-name identifiers as acceptable because it first accepts identical tokens, then only validates differing IDENT oldName -> newName tokens. A diff like want.OldField -> want.NewField while leaving got.OldField unchanged still sets renameSeen and passes, even though it is not a pure rename everywhere in the predicate.
findings.recommendation: Reject any remaining oldName token on the added side and require every oldName occurrence in removed tokens to map to newName at the same position.

findings.title: compile_fix_only AST check ignores comment directives
findings.evidence: compile-fix-scope-check skips comment lines before scope validation. That means a comment-only change is invisible to the AST gate; if the regex path is satisfied by mentioning the scoped symbol, a semantic directive such as //go:build Reconcile can disable a test file under a compile_fix_only exception.
findings.recommendation: Do not blanket-skip comments. Reject Go directives and build constraints in compile_fix_only, or require comment changes to pass the same scoped-symbol and directive-safety checks as code lines.

findings.title: audit truncation check counts grants but does not verify exception IDs
findings.evidence: The hook only compares grep-counted granted events with the number of approved exceptions. A log containing grants for E-001 and E-999 passes for an artifact approving E-001 and E-002, so the missing E-002 grant is not detected.
findings.recommendation: Use jq to compare the set of approved exception IDs for the cycle against granted audit events for the same cycle; fail closed on any missing approved ID, duplicate ambiguity, or malformed event.
